Category Description (from Tech Job Cate: A step-by-step process to carry out work.
Basic Definition of the Method:
The Google Design Sprint Method is a way to quickly solve problems and test new ideas in just a few days. It's like a fast-paced workshop where a team works together to come up with solutions and see if they work. This method is part of something called Agile, which is all about being flexible and making changes quickly to improve things.

Google Design Sprints are part of a group of methods in Agile. Agile methods are all about being flexible and working together as a team to get things done quickly and efficiently. They help teams adapt to changes and improve their work continuously.
The Origination of the Method:
The Google Design Sprint Method was created by a guy named Jake Knapp and his team at Google. They wanted to find a way to solve big problems quickly and test new ideas without spending too much time or money. Imagine trying to come up with a new game or app in just a few days instead of months. That's what they were aiming for!

This method was developed because sometimes, when people work on projects, they can get stuck in endless meetings and planning. Jake and his team thought it would be cool to have a fun, fast-paced way to get everyone together, brainstorm lots of ideas, and quickly see which ones might actually work. It's like having a mini adventure where you explore different paths to find the best treasure.

Today, companies and teams use the Google Design Sprint Method to tackle all sorts of challenges. Whether they're designing a new product, improving a service, or just trying to solve a tricky problem, they gather their team, follow the steps, and come up with creative solutions. It's a bit like a team sport where everyone plays a part, and together, they can achieve something awesome in just a few days!
How Do You Start Using This Method on Te:
The Google Design Sprint Method is a way to quickly solve problems and test new ideas. It's like a fun, fast-paced workshop where a team works together to come up with solutions and see if they work. This method is part of something called Agile, which is all about being flexible and working in small, quick steps to get things done efficiently.

To carry out a Google Design Sprint, follow these steps: First, gather your team and decide on the problem you want to solve. Next, brainstorm lots of ideas and pick the best ones. Then, create a simple version of your idea, called a prototype, to see how it might work. After that, test your prototype with real people to get their feedback. Finally, look at what you learned from the testing and decide what to do next. Throughout the process, keep track of your progress and make sure everyone knows their role and responsibilities.
What Is The Process For The Method?:
Based on gv.com/sprint,  here's how you follow the process of the Google Design Sprint Method: 

Imagine you're on a mission to solve a big problem, like figuring out how to make the best new video game. The Google Design Sprint process is a 5 day process.

On Monday, you gather your team and decide what the game should achieve. 

Tuesday is all about brainstorming, where everyone sketches their coolest game ideas. 

By Wednesday, you pick the best ideas and create a storyboard, like planning the levels of your game. 

Thursday is when you build a simple version of the game, just enough to show how it works. 

Finally, on Friday, you let your friends play it and watch their reactions. This feedback helps you know what to improve, so you can make the game even better before launching it to the world.
Benefits of Using This Method: This method is unique because it allows teams to solve big problems and test new ideas in just five days. Unlike other agile methods like Scrum or Kanban, which focus on ongoing processes and continuous improvement, the Design Sprint is a short, focused activity. This method is like a fast-paced workshop where everyone works together to come up with creative solutions and see if they work. It helps teams quickly figure out if their ideas are good before spending a lot of time and resources on them. This is especially helpful for teams that need to innovate quickly and want to make sure their ideas are worth pursuing.
Weakness of Using The Method: The Google Design Sprint Method, while great for quickly testing new ideas, has some limitations. It's not the best choice for projects that need long-term planning and continuous improvement because it's designed for short, intense bursts of activity. Imagine trying to build a treehouse in a single weekend; you might get a basic structure up, but you won't have time to make it perfect or add all the cool features you want. Similarly, the Design Sprint is fantastic for getting a quick sense of whether an idea might work, but it doesn't allow for the ongoing adjustments and refinements that other methods like Scrum or Kanban offer. It's also not ideal for teams that need to work on complex projects over a long period, as it focuses on immediate results rather than sustained progress.
When Is This Method Used?: Teams should consider using the Google Design Sprint Method when they face a significant challenge or opportunity and need to find a solution quickly. It's ideal for situations where there is uncertainty about the best path forward, and the team needs to gather insights and feedback from real users before making big decisions. This method is also great for fostering team alignment and ensuring everyone is on the same page about the direction of a project.
How Can This Method Be Combined With Oth:
Teams can integrate the Google Design Sprint Method with other agile methods by leveraging the strengths of each approach. For instance, teams can use the Design Sprint for rapid prototyping and user testing, then transition to Scrum for iterative development and delivery. The Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) can be employed to align multiple teams working on large-scale projects, ensuring that insights from Design Sprints are incorporated into the broader product strategy. Kanban can be used to manage workflow and visualize tasks during the execution phase, while Continuous Discovery can be integrated to maintain ongoing user feedback and insights throughout the product lifecycle. Lean UX can complement Design Sprints by focusing on hypothesis-driven design and continuous learning, ensuring that user experience remains a priority.

To integrate other methods into the Google Design Sprint, roles can start by aligning the sprint outcomes with the backlog in Scrum, ensuring that prototypes and user feedback inform future sprints. In SAFe, roles can synchronize sprint results with program increments to maintain alignment across teams. For Kanban, roles can map sprint activities onto a Kanban board to track progress and manage bottlenecks. Continuous Discovery can be integrated by scheduling regular user interviews and testing sessions before and after sprints to gather insights. Lean UX can be incorporated by using sprint outcomes to validate assumptions and iterate on design solutions, fostering a culture of experimentation and learning.
How is This Agile Method Different from:
Unlike other agile methods like Scrum or Kanban, which focus on ongoing processes and continuous improvement, the Design Sprint is a short, time-boxed activity that usually lasts about five days. During this time, a team goes through a series of structured steps: understanding the problem, sketching solutions, deciding on the best ideas, building a prototype, and testing it with real users. This method is unique because it compresses months of work into a single week, allowing teams to rapidly validate ideas without committing to a long-term project.

The unique value of the Google Design Sprint Method lies in its ability to quickly generate and test ideas, reducing the risk of investing time and resources into a project that might not work. It encourages creativity and collaboration by bringing together people from different backgrounds to focus on a single challenge. This method is particularly valuable for teams that need to innovate quickly and want to ensure their ideas are viable before moving forward with development.

Category Description (from Tech Job Cate: A step-by-step process to carry out work.
Basic Definition of the Method: Scrum is a way of working together as a team to get things done more efficiently. It's like a game plan that helps everyone know what to do and when to do it. Scrum is part of a bigger group of methods called Agile, which is all about being flexible and ready to change plans if needed to make things better.
The Origination of the Method:
Scrum was created by two smart guys named Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland in the 1990s. They wanted to find a better way for teams to work together, especially when making software. They noticed that traditional methods were too slow and didn't allow for changes, so they came up with Scrum, which is like a playbook for teams to follow.

The reason they developed Scrum was to help teams be more flexible and efficient. Imagine trying to build a giant Lego set with your friends. If you all work together, share ideas, and adjust your plans when things don't fit, you can build it faster and better. That's what Scrum does for teams working on projects.

Today, Scrum is used by companies all over the world. Teams use it to plan their work in short bursts called sprints, where they focus on small tasks and meet regularly to talk about progress. This way, they can quickly see what's working and what needs to change, making it easier to deliver great results.
How Do You Start Using This Method on Te: To use Scrum, start by planning what you want to achieve in a short time, called a sprint. First, gather your team and decide what tasks need to be done. Then, during the sprint, keep track of how things are going and make sure everyone knows their roles. After the sprint, look back at what went well and what could be better. Show what you've done to others and get their feedback. Finally, make sure everyone agrees on how to work together and help each other improve.
What Is The Process For The Method?:
Based on https://scrum.org, here's how you follow the process of the Scrum method: 

Imagine you're playing a team sport like soccer, where everyone has a role, and you work together to score goals. 

In Scrum, your team is like that soccer team, but instead of scoring goals, you're completing projects. 

First, you have a Scrum Team, which includes a Product Owner, a Scrum Master, and Developers. They work together in short cycles called Sprints, which are like mini-games lasting up to a month. 

During each Sprint, the team plans what they can achieve, works on it, and then reviews what they've done to see how they can improve. It's all about teamwork, trying new things, and learning from what works and what doesn't. 

Just like in soccer, where you might change your strategy based on how the game is going, in Scrum, you adapt your plans based on feedback and results. This way, you keep getting better and delivering valuable work, just like scoring more goals in each game.
Benefits of Using This Method: This method is unique because it gives your team a clear structure with specific roles like the Scrum Master and Product Owner, which helps everyone stay on track. Unlike other methods like Kanban, which focuses on visualizing work without set roles or timeframes, Scrum uses short cycles called Sprints to deliver small, usable parts of a project quickly. This allows the team to get feedback and make improvements regularly, just like adjusting your strategy in a game. The routine and rhythm of Scrum make it easier to predict and manage work, leading to faster delivery and better teamwork, as everyone knows their responsibilities and can see the project's progress clearly.
Weakness of Using The Method: Scrum might not be the best fit for teams that need a lot of flexibility or have projects with constantly changing requirements. For example, if you're working on a creative project like designing a new video game, where ideas and features might change frequently, the strict structure of Scrum with its fixed roles and timeframes can feel too rigid. It can also be challenging for very small teams or projects that don't have enough people to fill all the specific roles like Scrum Master and Product Owner. Additionally, if a team is not fully committed to the Scrum process, it can lead to frustration and inefficiency, as the method relies heavily on everyone following the rules and participating actively.
When Is This Method Used?: Teams should consider using Scrum when they need a clear framework to manage complex projects with many moving parts. It's especially useful when the team benefits from regular feedback and needs to adapt quickly to changes. If a team thrives on routine and clear roles, Scrum can provide the structure needed to keep everyone on track and motivated.
How Can This Method Be Combined With Oth:
Teams can integrate Scrum with the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) by using Scrum for team-level activities while adopting SAFe for coordination across multiple teams. UX Designers and Researchers can combine Scrum with Lean UX by using Lean UX principles to rapidly test and iterate on designs within the Scrum sprints. Developers can integrate Scrum with Kanban by using Scrum for sprint planning and Kanban for managing work in progress during the sprint. Solutions Architects can use Scrum with Continuous Discovery by incorporating ongoing discovery activities into the Scrum backlog refinement process. Project Managers can blend Scrum with the Google Design Sprint by using Design Sprints for rapid prototyping and problem-solving within the Scrum framework. Agile Coaches can facilitate the integration of Scrum with other methods by providing guidance on best practices and ensuring alignment with agile principles. Business Analysts can combine Scrum with Continuous Discovery by continuously gathering and refining requirements within the Scrum process. Product Owners and Managers can integrate Scrum with Lean UX by prioritizing user-centered design and feedback within the Scrum backlog.

To integrate other methods into Scrum, roles can take specific steps: Scrum Masters can facilitate workshops to align teams on the integration strategy. UX Designers and Researchers can conduct user testing sessions and incorporate findings into the Scrum backlog. Developers can implement Kanban boards to visualize work in progress and manage flow within sprints. Solutions Architects can collaborate with Product Owners to ensure discovery activities are aligned with sprint goals. Project Managers can schedule Design Sprints at the beginning of a project phase to inform the Scrum backlog. Agile Coaches can provide training sessions on the new methods being integrated. Business Analysts can work closely with stakeholders to ensure continuous discovery is reflected in the backlog. Product Owners and Managers can prioritize tasks that align with Lean UX principles, ensuring that user feedback is continuously integrated into product development.
How is This Agile Method Different from:
Unlike other agile methods, Scrum has a clear structure with defined roles like the Scrum Master, Product Owner, and the development team. It focuses on delivering small, usable pieces of a project quickly and getting feedback to improve in the next sprint. This is different from methods like Kanban, which is more about visualizing work and managing flow without fixed roles or timeframes, or the Scaled Agile Framework, which is designed for coordinating large teams across an organization.

The unique value of Scrum lies in its ability to create a rhythm and routine for teams, making it easier to predict and manage work. It encourages regular check-ins and adjustments, which helps teams stay aligned and focused on their goals. This structured approach can lead to faster delivery of products and better team collaboration, as everyone knows their responsibilities and the project’s progress is transparent.

Category Description (from Tech Job Cate: A step-by-step process to carry out work.
Basic Definition of the Method:
The Scaled Agile Framework, or SAFe, is a way to help big teams work together better. It's like a set of rules and ideas that guide everyone on how to plan, work, and improve as a team. Agile is a way of working that focuses on being flexible and quick, so teams can make changes easily and deliver their work faster.

Scaled Agile Framework (AKA SAFe) is part of a group of methods in Agile. Agile methods are all about being flexible and working together as a team to get things done quickly and efficiently. They help teams adapt to changes and improve their work continuously.
The Origination of the Method:
The Scaled Agile Framework, or SAFe, was created by a group of people who wanted to help big teams work better together. It all started when Dean Leffingwell, a software expert, noticed that large companies were struggling to keep up with changes and deliver projects on time. He and his team came up with a set of guidelines to help these companies be more flexible and efficient, just like smaller teams that use Agile methods.

SAFe was developed because big companies needed a way to be quick and adaptable, like a sports team that needs to change its strategy during a game. By using SAFe, companies can plan better, work faster, and improve continuously. It's like having a playbook that helps everyone know their role and how to work together smoothly.

Today, many companies use SAFe to help their teams stay organized and productive. It's like a roadmap that guides them on how to plan their work, check their progress, and make improvements. Teams use SAFe to make sure everyone knows what to do, how to help each other, and how to solve problems together. This way, they can deliver their projects on time and keep their customers happy.
How Do You Start Using This Method on Te:
The Scaled Agile Framework Method is a way for teams to work together more effectively, especially when they have big projects. It's like a set of rules and practices that help everyone stay organized and on the same page. This method is part of a bigger group of ways to work called Agile, which focuses on being flexible and making changes quickly when needed.

To use this method, teams follow several steps. First, they plan their work in a meeting called Sprint Planning, where they decide what tasks to do next. After working for a set time, they have a Sprint Retrospective to talk about what went well and what could be better. They also have a Sprint Demo to show what they've done. Throughout the project, they keep track of their progress and refine their requirements to make sure they're on the right track. Teams also define roles and responsibilities, set team agreements, and establish a process for how they will work together. Finally, they support each other by coaching team members to improve their skills.
What Is The Process For The Method?:
Based on https://scaledagileframework.com/, here's how you follow the process of the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) method: 

Imagine you're organizing a big group project at school. 

First, you gather everyone to decide what needs to be done, just like planning a party where you assign who brings snacks or games. This is called Sprint Planning. 

Next, after working on your tasks, you meet again to discuss what went well and what could be improved, similar to talking about how the party went and what could be better next time—this is the Sprint Retrospective. 

Then, you show your work to others, like presenting your project to the class, which is the Sprint Demo. 

Throughout, you keep track of progress to ensure everyone is on track, like checking if everyone has done their part for the party. You also refine requirements by making sure everyone knows their tasks clearly, like ensuring everyone knows what snacks to bring. 

Finally, you define roles and set team agreements, like deciding who is responsible for what and how you'll communicate, ensuring everyone works together smoothly.
Benefits of Using This Method: This method is unique because unlike other agile methods that might be better for smaller groups, SAFe is perfect for big companies with lots of teams. It helps everyone stay on the same page by setting clear roles and responsibilities, just like making sure everyone knows their job at the event. SAFe is great for breaking down barriers between different groups, so everyone works towards the same goal. This is especially helpful for big projects with many parts, as it allows the whole company to be flexible and adapt to changes while still moving in the right direction.
Weakness of Using The Method: The Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) is not always the best fit for smaller teams or companies because it can be too complex and rigid for their needs. Imagine trying to organize a small group of friends for a simple project, but using a detailed plan meant for a huge event with hundreds of people. It might feel like overkill and slow things down instead of speeding them up. SAFe requires a lot of coordination and planning, which can be overwhelming for smaller teams that thrive on flexibility and quick decision-making. Additionally, it can be challenging to implement in organizations that aren't already familiar with agile practices, as it demands a significant cultural shift and commitment from everyone involved.
When Is This Method Used?: Teams should consider using SAFe when they are part of a large organization that needs to coordinate efforts across multiple departments or when they are dealing with complex projects that require input from various teams. It's especially useful when there is a need for alignment and collaboration at a large scale, ensuring that everyone is moving in the same direction and can respond quickly to changes in the market or customer needs.
How Can This Method Be Combined With Oth:
Teams using the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) can integrate it with other agile methods by leveraging the strengths of each role. Scrum Masters can facilitate the integration of Scrum by using its ceremonies for planning and retrospectives, while Kanban can be used for visualizing work in progress and managing flow. UX Designers and Researchers can incorporate Lean UX principles to ensure user-centered design is embedded in the development process, while Google Design Sprints can be used for rapid prototyping and testing. Developers can adopt Continuous Discovery practices to iteratively gather feedback and refine features. Solutions Architects can align architectural decisions with Lean principles to optimize efficiency. Project Managers and Agile Coaches can use Kanban to manage project flow and ensure continuous improvement. Business Analysts can employ Continuous Discovery to validate assumptions and refine requirements. Product Owners and Managers can use Lean UX and Continuous Discovery to ensure the product vision aligns with user needs and market demands.

To integrate other methods into SAFe, roles can take specific steps: Scrum Masters can introduce Kanban boards to visualize work and manage flow alongside Scrum ceremonies. UX Designers and Researchers can conduct Lean UX workshops and Google Design Sprints to align design efforts with user needs. Developers can implement Continuous Discovery by setting up regular feedback loops with stakeholders. Solutions Architects can apply Lean principles to streamline architecture decisions. Project Managers and Agile Coaches can use Kanban metrics to track progress and identify bottlenecks. Business Analysts can engage in Continuous Discovery activities to refine user stories and requirements. Product Owners and Managers can incorporate Lean UX and Continuous Discovery insights into the product backlog to ensure alignment with user and business goals.
How is This Agile Method Different from:
Unlike other agile methods that might focus on smaller teams or specific tasks, SAFe is designed to coordinate multiple teams across a whole company. It provides a structured approach to ensure everyone is on the same page, with clear roles and responsibilities, and it emphasizes planning at different levels to align the entire organization towards common goals.

SAFe brings unique value by allowing big companies to be agile, which means they can adapt quickly to changes while still maintaining a clear direction. It helps in breaking down silos between departments, ensuring that everyone is working towards the same objectives. This method is particularly valuable for organizations that need to manage complex projects with many moving parts, as it provides a framework to scale agile practices across the entire enterprise.

Category Description (from Tech Job Cate: A step-by-step process to carry out work.
Basic Definition of the Method:
The Kanban Method is a way to organize and manage work by using a visual board. Imagine a big board with columns that show different stages of a task, like "To Do," "In Progress," and "Done." You move tasks through these columns as you work on them. This helps everyone see what needs to be done and what is already finished. 

Kanban is part of a group of methods in Agile. Agile methods are all about being flexible and working together as a team to get things done quickly and efficiently. They help teams adapt to changes and improve their work continuously.
The Origination of the Method:
The Kanban Method started in Japan at a car company called Toyota. A smart engineer named Taiichi Ohno came up with the idea in the 1940s. He wanted to make the car-making process smoother and faster, so he created a system that used cards to show what parts were needed and when. This helped the workers know exactly what to do next, making everything run more efficiently.

The reason Kanban was developed was to solve a big problem: how to keep track of all the tasks and parts needed to build cars without getting confused or wasting time. By using a simple visual system, everyone could see what was happening and what needed to be done. It was like having a giant to-do list that everyone could understand at a glance, making teamwork easier and more fun.

Today, the Kanban Method is used by companies and teams all over the world, not just in car factories. It's like a magic board that helps people organize their work, whether they're building software, planning events, or even doing school projects. Teams use it to see what tasks are in progress, what still needs to be done, and what is already finished. This way, everyone stays on the same page, and work gets done more smoothly and quickly.
How Do You Start Using This Method on Te:
The Kanban Method is a way to organize and manage work by using visual boards. It helps teams see what needs to be done, what is in progress, and what is finished. This method is part of a bigger group called Agile, which is all about being flexible and making changes quickly to improve how things are done.

To use the Kanban Method, start by making a board with columns for tasks that need to be done, tasks that are in progress, and tasks that are completed. First, gather your team and list all the tasks you need to work on. Then, write each task on a card and place it in the "To Do" column. As you start working on a task, move its card to the "In Progress" column. When a task is finished, move its card to the "Done" column. Keep checking the board to see how work is moving along and make changes if needed to keep everything running smoothly.
What Is The Process For The Method?:
Based on the https://kanban.university/kanban-guide/, here's how you follow the process of the Kanban Method: 

Imagine you're organizing your schoolwork using a big board. 

First, you create columns labeled "To Do," "In Progress," and "Done." Each task, like homework or a project, starts in the "To Do" column. 

As you begin working on a task, you move it to "In Progress," and once it's completed, it goes to "Done." This visual setup helps you see what needs attention and what's already finished. 

The key steps include visualizing your tasks, limiting how many tasks you work on at once to avoid feeling overwhelmed, and managing the flow of tasks to ensure they move smoothly from start to finish. 

By making your work visible and setting clear rules, you can improve how you manage your time and tasks, just like a team using Kanban to handle projects efficiently.
Benefits of Using This Method: This method is unique because unlike other agile methods that might have specific roles or meetings, Kanban focuses on visualizing tasks on a board, which helps everyone see what needs to be done and what is already finished. This method is great for managing a steady flow of work because it helps you spot and fix problems quickly. By limiting how many tasks you work on at once, Kanban prevents you from feeling overwhelmed and helps you prioritize tasks effectively. This makes it especially useful for teams that need to adapt quickly to changes.
Weakness of Using The Method: The Kanban Method, while great for visualizing work and managing a steady flow, might not be the best fit for every team. For example, if a team needs a lot of structure and clear roles, Kanban might feel too loose because it doesn't have specific roles or regular meetings like some other methods. Imagine trying to play a team sport without set positions or a game plan; it might get confusing. Also, if a team is working on a big project with a strict deadline, Kanban's focus on continuous flow might not provide enough guidance on how to plan and track progress towards that deadline. It's like trying to finish a school project without a clear timeline or checkpoints, which can make it hard to know if you're on track.
When Is This Method Used?: Teams should consider using Kanban when they want to improve their workflow without overhauling their current processes. It’s ideal for environments where work comes in a steady stream, like support or maintenance teams, and where flexibility and quick adjustments are necessary. Kanban is also great for teams that prefer a less structured approach and want to focus on visualizing and managing their work efficiently.
How Can This Method Be Combined With Oth:
Teams can integrate Kanban with other agile methods by leveraging the strengths of each role and method. UX Designers and Researchers can combine Kanban with the Lean UX Method by using Kanban boards to visualize and manage the flow of design tasks while applying Lean UX principles to iterate quickly based on user feedback. UX Writers can use Kanban alongside the Continuous Discovery Method to ensure content development aligns with ongoing user research insights. Developers and Solutions Architects can integrate Kanban with Scrum by using Kanban for continuous delivery and Scrum for structured sprint planning. Project Managers and Agile Coaches can blend Kanban with the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) to manage work-in-progress limits while coordinating across multiple teams. Business Analysts, Product Owners, and Product Managers can use Kanban with the Google Design Sprint Method to manage the flow of ideas and prototypes, ensuring rapid iteration and feedback.

To integrate other methods into Kanban, roles can take specific steps. UX roles can start by mapping their workflow on a Kanban board, then incorporate Lean UX practices by setting up regular feedback loops and experiments. Developers can maintain a Kanban board for ongoing tasks while using Scrum ceremonies like sprint reviews to plan and reflect. Project Managers can implement SAFe by aligning Kanban boards across teams to visualize dependencies and manage cross-team collaboration. Product roles can use the Google Design Sprint framework to structure ideation and testing phases within the Kanban system, ensuring that each stage of the sprint is visible and manageable. By clearly defining workflows and regularly reviewing progress, teams can effectively integrate these methods with Kanban.
How is This Agile Method Different from:
Unlike other agile methods that might have set roles and meetings, Kanban is flexible and focuses on visualizing work, limiting work in progress, and improving flow. It uses a board with columns to represent different stages of work, making it easy for everyone to understand what’s happening at a glance.

The unique value of Kanban is its simplicity and adaptability. It doesn’t require teams to change their existing processes drastically. Instead, it helps them gradually improve by identifying bottlenecks and inefficiencies. This makes it particularly useful for teams that need to manage a continuous flow of work, as it allows them to respond quickly to changes and prioritize tasks effectively.

Category Description (from Tech Job Cate: A step-by-step process to carry out work.
Basic Definition of the Method:
Continuous Discovery Method is a way of constantly learning about what users need and want. It's like being a detective who is always on the lookout for clues about how to make a product better. This method is part of something called Agile, which is a flexible way of working that helps teams quickly adapt to changes and improve their work.

Continuous Discovery is part of a group of methods in Agile. Agile methods are all about being flexible and working together as a team to get things done quickly and efficiently. They help teams adapt to changes and improve their work continuously.
The Origination of the Method:
The Continuous Discovery Method was created by Theresa Torres and a group who wanted to help teams make better products by always learning what users need. Imagine being a detective who never stops looking for clues to solve a mystery. These detectives, or creators, realized that by constantly asking questions and gathering information, they could help companies make things that people really want and need.

This method was developed because the creators noticed that sometimes companies would make products that didn't quite fit what people were looking for. By using this method, teams can keep up with what users want, just like how you might change your playlist based on what songs you feel like listening to. It's all about staying flexible and ready to adapt to new information.

Today, companies use the Continuous Discovery Method to make sure they're always on the right track with their products. Teams work together like a group of friends planning a surprise party, constantly checking in with each other and the guests to make sure everything is just right. This way, they can quickly change plans if needed and make sure everyone is happy with the final result.
How Do You Start Using This Method on Te:
The Continuous Discovery Method is a way of working where teams constantly learn and discover new things to improve their products or services. It's part of a bigger way of working called Agile, which means being flexible and ready to change plans as you learn more.

To use this method, start by coming up with lots of ideas quickly (Rapid Ideation). Then, plan a short period of work called a sprint (Sprint Planning). After the sprint, look back at what went well and what could be better (Sprint Retrospective Facilitation). Show what you've done to others (Sprint Demo Facilitation) and keep track of how things are going (Tracking Sprint Progress). Learn from research by making plans, talking to people, and testing ideas (Analyzing Research Findings, Creating Research Plans, Interviewing, Surveys, Prototype Concept Testing, A/B Prototype Testing). Make simple versions of your ideas to test them (Prototype Creation, Clickable Prototype Creation). Help your team learn and grow (Coaching Team Mates). Design experiments to test your ideas (Experiment Design) and study the market and customers (Market Research, Secondary Research). Share what you find out (Presenting Research Results) and look for patterns in your research (Identifying Research Patterns). Guide research activities (Moderating UX Research, Contextual Inquiries, Observing, Diary Studies) and decide what’s most important (Identifying Priorities). Analyze how easy things are to use (UX Heuristics Analysis) and plan how to introduce your product to the market (Developing Go-To-Market Strategy). Understand different types of customers (Customer Segmentation) and map out their experiences (Customer Experience Mapping). Create a unique value for your product (Unique Value Proposition Formation) and define your overall plan (Strategy Definition).
What Is The Process For The Method?:
Based on the https://www.interaction-design.org/, here's how you follow the process of the Continuous Discovery Method: 

Imagine you're a detective, always on the lookout for clues to make a product better. 

This method involves regularly talking to users, like having a weekly chat with friends to see what they like or dislike about a game you're developing. Instead of doing all your research at once, you gather small bits of information continuously, like collecting puzzle pieces over time. This way, you can quickly adapt and improve your product based on real feedback. 

The key is to keep a steady schedule, like interviewing a few users every week, and involve your whole team in the process. This helps everyone understand the users better and make smarter decisions together.
Benefits of Using This Method: This method is unique because unlike other agile methods like Lean UX, Google Design Sprint, or Scrum, which might focus on specific phases or have set time frames, Continuous Discovery is all about ongoing learning. This means you regularly talk to users, like having weekly chats to see what they think about your product. By doing this, you can make small changes often, instead of waiting for a big reveal at the end. This approach helps ensure that what you're creating is exactly what users want and need, reducing the risk of making something that doesn't meet their expectations. It allows teams to quickly fix any issues based on real feedback, leading to a product that is more likely to succeed.
Weakness of Using The Method: The Continuous Discovery Method, while great for keeping up with what users want, might not be the best fit for every team or situation. One limitation is that it requires a lot of time and effort to constantly gather and analyze user feedback, which can be overwhelming for smaller teams with limited resources. It's like trying to keep up with a never-ending stream of homework assignments without a break. Additionally, this method might not work well for projects with strict deadlines or where the scope is already clearly defined, as the ongoing changes could lead to delays. For teams that need a more structured approach with clear phases and timelines, other agile methods like Scrum might be more suitable.
When Is This Method Used?: Teams should consider using the Continuous Discovery Method when they want to stay closely connected to their users and ensure that their product evolves based on real-world insights. It's particularly useful for projects where user needs might change frequently or are not fully understood at the start. This method is ideal for teams that value flexibility and are willing to adapt their plans based on ongoing learning and discovery.
How Can This Method Be Combined With Oth:
Teams can integrate the Continuous Discovery Method with other agile methods by leveraging the strengths of each role. UX Designers and Researchers can combine Continuous Discovery with Lean UX by using ongoing user feedback to inform design iterations, ensuring that user needs are continuously addressed. UX Writers can integrate Google Design Sprint by crafting clear, concise content during rapid prototyping phases, enhancing communication and user understanding. Product Owners and Managers can blend Continuous Discovery with Scrum by using discovery insights to prioritize backlogs and refine user stories, ensuring that development aligns with user needs. Agile Coaches can facilitate the integration of Continuous Discovery with the Scaled Agile Framework by promoting a culture of continuous learning and adaptation across teams, ensuring that discovery insights are shared and implemented at scale. Kanban can be used alongside Continuous Discovery by visualizing and managing the flow of discovery tasks, ensuring a steady stream of insights into the development process.

To integrate other methods into the Continuous Discovery Method, roles can take specific steps. UX Designers and Researchers can start by incorporating Lean UX principles, such as hypothesis-driven design and rapid experimentation, into their discovery activities. UX Writers can participate in Google Design Sprints to quickly develop and test content strategies that align with user insights. Product Owners and Managers can use Scrum ceremonies, like sprint reviews and retrospectives, to incorporate discovery findings into planning and prioritization. Agile Coaches can guide teams in adopting Scaled Agile Framework practices, such as PI (Program Increment) planning, to align discovery efforts with broader organizational goals. Kanban can be integrated by setting up visual boards to track discovery tasks, ensuring that insights are continuously fed into the development pipeline.
How is This Agile Method Different from:
Unlike other agile methods like Lean UX, Google Design Sprint, or Scrum, which might focus on specific phases or time-boxed activities, Continuous Discovery is an ongoing process. It emphasizes regular interactions with users to understand their needs and challenges, allowing teams to make small, frequent adjustments rather than waiting for a big reveal at the end.

This method brings unique value by ensuring that the product or service being developed is always aligned with what users actually want and need. By continuously engaging with users, teams can quickly identify and address any issues, leading to a product that is more likely to succeed in the real world. This approach reduces the risk of building something that doesn't meet user expectations, as adjustments are made in real-time based on actual feedback.

Category Description (from Tech Job Cate: A step-by-step process to carry out work.
Basic Definition of the Method:
Lean UX is a way of working that helps teams quickly come up with ideas and test them to make sure they are useful and easy to use. It's all about working together, trying things out, and learning from what works and what doesn't. This method is part of a bigger way of working called Agile, which means being flexible and ready to change plans as you learn new things.

Lean UX is part of a group of methods in Agile. Agile methods are all about being flexible and working together as a team to get things done quickly and efficiently. They help teams adapt to changes and improve their work continuously.
The Origination of the Method:
Lean UX was created by a guy named Jeff Gothelf. He made this because he wanted to make designing products faster and more fun for everyone involved. He noticed that when people worked together and shared ideas, they could come up with better solutions more quickly. So, he developed Lean UX to help teams brainstorm, test, and improve their ideas without getting stuck in long, boring processes.

This method was developed because Jeff saw that traditional ways of designing things were too slow and didn't always lead to the best results. By encouraging teams to be flexible and open to change, Lean UX helps them learn from their mistakes and make better products. It's like when you're working on a school project and you keep trying different things until you find what works best.

Today, companies and teams use Lean UX to create everything from apps to websites. They start by coming up with ideas, then quickly test them to see if they work. If something doesn't work, they change it and try again. It's like baking cookies and tasting them to see if they're good, then adjusting the recipe until they're perfect. This way, teams can make sure their products are useful and enjoyable for everyone.
How Do You Start Using This Method on Te:
Lean UX is a way of working that helps teams create better products by focusing on what users need and want. It's part of a bigger way of working called Agile, which is all about being flexible and working together to get things done quickly and efficiently.

To use Lean UX, start by coming up with lots of ideas quickly (Rapid Ideation). Then, plan what you will do in a short period of time, called a sprint (Sprint Planning). After the sprint, look back at what went well and what could be better (Sprint Retrospective Facilitation). Show what you've done to others (Sprint Demo Facilitation) and keep track of how things are going (Tracking Sprint Progress). Learn from research by making plans, talking to people, and testing ideas (Analyzing Research Findings, Creating Research Plans, Interviewing, Surveys, Prototype Concept Testing, A/B Prototype Testing). Make simple models of your ideas (Prototype Creation, Clickable Prototype Creation) and figure out what problems need solving (Generating Problem Statements). Help your team understand their roles and how to work together (Coaching Team Mates, Team Role and Responsibility Definition, Defining Team Agreements, Defining Team Process). Keep learning from what you see and hear (Observing, Diary Studies, Contextual Inquiries) and share what you find out (Presenting Research Results).
What Is The Process For The Method?:
Based on the https://www.interaction-design.org/, here's how you follow the process of the Lean UX method: 

Imagine you're working on a group project at school, and you want to make sure your idea is the best it can be. 

First, instead of writing a long list of rules, you start with a simple problem statement, like "How can we make our school app easier to use?" 

Then, you and your friends brainstorm ideas and make assumptions, which are like educated guesses about what might work. 

Next, you create a hypothesis, which is a fancy way of saying, "We think if we add a feature to save progress, more students will finish using the app." 

You test this by building a basic version of the app, called a Minimum Viable Product (MVP), and see if it works. 

If it does, great! If not, you tweak it and try again. This way, you're always learning and improving, just like in a video game where you keep trying different strategies to beat a level.
Benefits of Using This Method: This method is unique because it focuses on experimentation in the user experience process. Imagine working on a school project where everyone shares ideas and tests them right away to see what works best. Lean UX encourages teams to do just that—work closely together, try out ideas early, and learn from real user feedback. This helps create products that truly meet user needs because the team is always testing and improving based on what users actually want. By involving everyone and focusing on user experience, Lean UX helps build better products faster and avoids wasting time on features that aren't needed.
Weakness of Using The Method: Lean UX might not be the best fit for teams that need strict timelines and detailed planning. Imagine if you had a school project where you needed to know exactly what everyone was doing each day to finish on time. Lean UX focuses more on trying out ideas and learning from them, which can make it hard to stick to a set schedule. It also might not work well for teams that aren't used to working closely together or for projects that require a lot of upfront planning and documentation, like building a bridge or designing a new car. In these cases, the lack of detailed plans and the focus on constant change can be challenging.
When Is This Method Used?: Teams should consider using Lean UX when they want to create user-centered products and are open to changing direction based on feedback. It's especially useful for projects where understanding the user's needs is crucial, and the team is willing to experiment and iterate quickly. If a team values collaboration and wants to ensure their product is aligned with what users actually want, Lean UX is a great choice.
How Can This Method Be Combined With Oth:
Teams can integrate Lean UX with other Agile methods by using sprints for rapid prototyping and testing, then applying Lean UX principles to iterate based on user feedback. UX Researchers can combine Lean UX with Continuous Discovery by continuously gathering user insights and feeding them into Lean UX cycles for ongoing improvement. UX Writers can use Lean UX alongside Scrum by aligning content creation with sprint goals and iterating based on user testing results. Product Owners and Managers can integrate Lean UX with the Scaled Agile Framework by incorporating Lean UX practices into the larger framework to ensure user-centered design at scale. Agile Coaches can facilitate the integration of Lean UX with Kanban by using Kanban boards to visualize and manage Lean UX tasks, ensuring a smooth flow of work and continuous delivery.

To integrate other methods into Lean UX, UX Designers can start by incorporating Google Design Sprint techniques for initial ideation and prototyping, then transition to Lean UX for iterative development. UX Researchers can establish a continuous feedback loop by embedding Continuous Discovery practices into Lean UX cycles. UX Writers can align their work with Scrum sprints, ensuring content is developed iteratively and tested with users. Product Owners and Managers can adapt Lean UX within the Scaled Agile Framework by aligning Lean UX activities with program increments and release trains. Agile Coaches can guide teams in using Kanban to manage Lean UX tasks, ensuring a steady flow of work and quick adaptation to user feedback.
How is This Agile Method Different from:
Unlike other agile methods like Scrum or Kanban, which might focus more on managing tasks and timelines, Lean UX emphasizes collaboration and experimentation. It encourages teams to work together closely, test ideas early, and learn from real user interactions to improve the product continuously.

The unique value of Lean UX lies in its ability to adapt and evolve based on user feedback. This approach helps teams create products that truly meet user needs because they are constantly testing and refining ideas. By focusing on user experience and involving everyone in the process, Lean UX helps teams build better products faster and with less waste, as they avoid spending time on features that users don't want or need.
